title_tesim: Pitcher plant, Los Angeles, [1931?]
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2536, 12, Orchids.  BBEO,  Pitcher Plant.  Medici of the vegetable kingdom--here is the odd pitcher plant whose poison sacks kill insects.  Photo shows: A picture of the pitcher plant--[unclear] from its odd shape.  This plant (Nepenthes Tiveyii) grows near orchids in tropical jungles.  It has poison sacks from 3 to 5 inches long on the ends of its leaves.  Insects wandering into these poison sacks are killed.  [11?]3B1694 PITCHER PLANT.  [Struck out: 113B1694]

title_tesim: View towards beach house under construction in the Rancho Malibu la Costa development, Malibu, circa 1927
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Downward view from slope towards house under construction along the inland side of the Pacific Coast Highway in the Rancho Malibu la Costa development on the Malibu coast. Two houses are visible on the beach side of the highway.

Rancho Topanga Malibu Sequit was a 13,316-acre Spanish land grant given by Spanish Governor José Joaquín de Arrillaga in 1804 to José Bartolomé Tapia. In 1892 Frederick Hastings Rindge and May Knight Rindge, purchased the rancho. In 1926, the Marblehead Land Company, formed by May Knight Rindge, made a $6 million deal with Harold G. Ferguson to develop the La Costa area (between Las Flores Canyon and Carbon Canyon), but that deal fell apart when Ferguson was sentenced to jail for securities fraud in 1931. Ferguson laid out the development with street names as they remain today: Rambla Vista, Paseo Serra, etc. It took until the mid 1930's for development of the area to begin in earnest.

title_tesim: Newspaper clipping headed Prehistoric Indians' Works Found in Nevada Desert
caption_tesim: Text reads: Las Vegas, Nev., May 17.--(AP)--Discovery in a desert wilderness northwest of here of traces of Indian life believed extinct for at least 30 centuries prompted J. L. Nusmaum, Government archaeologist to telegraph Washington today recommending establishment of a national monument. The most prized discoveries were made in the Valley f Fire. Carvings believed wrought by the oldest known human race in America were found on the striking and fantastic rock formations of the valley. Particular importance was attached to writings on one cliff, which depicted a mountain sheep hunt. The remarkable feature, Nusbaum declared, was a drawing of hunters armed with “atlatis” peculiar weapons sought for years by archaeologists but never seen before. From this weapon, it was believed, later Indian races evolved the bow and arrow.  Leaving the valley, the party proceeded to the little known petrified forest. Here were found gigantic trees which had been swept to earth as though felled by a huge scythe. By the slow process of immersion in silicified water, they have been turned into agate.

subject_tesim: Will Rogers State Beach (Los Angeles, Calif.)
description_tesim: Text reads in part: Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica.  281R1694, 287R1694, 286R1694, 285R1694, 284R1694.  NOTE: See copies of newspaper stories attached in re McCormick family member of which is Will Roger's next door neighbor in story below -- Will Rogers private beach and "lighthouse" bathouse, cafe, soft drink and hot dog stand, below Huntington Palisades, Santa Monica (Los Angeles beach), California, will shortly pass into ownership of the County of Los Angeles, if plans of supervisors in their effort to acquire more privately owned beach lands for public use go through ... Will's beach has been free to all comers. ... Will has for immediate neighbors Miss Virginia McCormick and her large staff ... on the choicest building plot ... relatives all multimillionaire heirs of the McCormick harvester millions.  (see negatives enclosed)  The McCormick estate stands high and secluded ... tightly-locked gates ... Will Roger's open-handedness in making the public at home on his beach near-by is in striking contrast with the McCormick situation mentioned above.

title_tesim: Biplane in air, photographed from below, Tanforan Racetrack, San Bruno, 1911
caption_tesim: Handwritten at edge of negative: 2033

[Stamped:] Photo by Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, Calif.  [Typewritten:] Photographed 1910 by Adelbert Bartlett.  Exclusive unpublished photographs of one of first important Pacific Coast airplane meet, Tanforan field, San Francisco, January 1911, with Lincoln Beachy and other pilots flying.  At this meet were brought out many queer planes.  The majority made only short flights even if they managed to get off ground; many were wrecked.  [Handwritten:] Just 21 years ago.  12 each 6x8.  14 6x8.  [Handwrtten, struck out: 260R1694, 261R1694] [Some text torn: possibly 2034, 2035, 2036]

description_tesim: Article: "Overland Trek Commemorated; Pioneers Parade on Anniversary of Historic Journey," Los Angeles Times, 24 Jul. 1930: A1.

Article torn from newspaper, with two bird's-eye view photographs of Santa Monica Pioneer Days parade.  Text reads, in part: Santa Monica, bedecked with flags and flowers, was host yesterday to hundreds of California pioneers who gathered to march in a quaint parade in commemoration of the 100th anniversary of the first trek of pioneers from St. Louis to the Pacific Coast.  The parade depicted the development of transportation from pioneer days to the present, and covered wagons drawn by oxen, with airplanes flying overhead, made a striking picture.  Frank M. Hauser, grand marshal … Henry Englebrecht, Guy Bundy, and William Sharples … Mrs. Mary Sheckles … T.C. Hull … Miss Josephine Potter … the Santa Monica Municipal Band, the Santa Monica Harmonica Band of 400 boys and girls, the Newsboys’ Band of the Santa Monica Outlook, the Boys’ Band of Inglewood, the police and fire departments of Santa Monica, and scores of pioneers in tallyhos, victorias, buckboards and other ancient vehicles. …

title_tesim: Marathon dance competitors preparing to start, Culver City, 1928

Dancers ready for starting gu[n.]  World's endurance dancing championship starting 5 pm on Washington boulevard in the motion picture studio center.  50 couples one-stepped six miles to Ocean Park amusem[ent] section where the contest will continue until completed. title_tesim: Clipper ship, Tweed, photographed reproduction, 1951
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: H 2969, New & Old Warships of U.S. Navy, copies of old & new & medieval models, 1951
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Reproduction of a painting of a vintage ship with numerous masts and flags rides body of water, probably ocean, with figure of man visible on deck.

Image is of the clipper ship, the Tweed, formerly named the Punjaub. The Punjaub was a sail/paddle steamer frigate built by the Indian navy. It was later purchased by Jock Willis, who changed her name to the Tweed and converted her to solely sail propulsion.
Photograph is page from book, caption at lower middle of page reads "TWEED". Below appears to read "From oilpainting by permission of Mr. L. Rothon", but has been cut off.

title_tesim: Dane Gorrell with 1888 horseshoe-shaped shell art wreath made by poet Ina Donna Coolbrith, Santa Monica, 1953
caption_tesim: Text from newspaper caption for simiar photograph: Shell Painting: Dane Gorrell with Poetess Ina Coolbrith's horseshoe design of California flowers modeled from sea shells interwoven with moss gathered at Santa Monica 65 years ago. Memento was treasure of late W. H. Perry family ["Heirloom Plaque Shows Poetess' Art," Los Angeles Times, October 12, 1953]
Text from negative sleeve: 2954 File B, Ina Coolbrith, Poetry Laureate, California 1915-1928, John Crum Collection, Miss Dane Gorrell of Marin County, model with plaque,  year 1953
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Dane Gorrell, with 1888 horse-shoe-shaped shell art wreath made by poet Ina Donna Coolbrith. The flowers represented include Mariposa lily, Shasta daisy, Copa de Oro, camelia, rose and other flowers. The wreath was a gift to Elizabeth Dalton Perry and William Hayes Perry. At the time of this photograph the wreath belonged to the Perrys' nephew John H. Crum of Santa Monica. The shell wreath is an example of Victorian era domestic crafts.

title_tesim: Architect John Lindsay in Windemere Hotel lobby, Santa Monica 1955
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: John Lindsay, architect, Sepulveda Blvd, 1955, Lady Windermere Hotel, Before Dean Train decorating.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: John Lindsay, southern California architect, stands in the lobby of the Hotel Windemere. Lindsay was associated with at least two the firms: Palmer, Krisel & Lindsay, A.I.A. for a brief time around 1952; and John C. Lindsay and Associates.

Lobby of Windemere Hotel, with the architect John Lindsay standing next to the mantel.  Other men are visible via the mirror that hangs over the mantel. Also visible are a sofa, coffee table and television, with a sign that reads "Public Television 10 cents 20 minutes".

title_tesim: Young girl with horseshoe-shaped wreath of shells made in 1888 by poet Ina Donna Coolbrith, 1953
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2954 File B Ina Coolbrith Poetry Laureate California 1915-1928 John Crum Collection Miss Dane Gorrell of Marin County model with plaque year 1953

The 1888 horse-shoe-shaped shell art wreath was composed of sea shells interwoven with moss gathered at Santa Monica. The flowers represented include Mariposa lily, Shasta daisy, Copa de Oro, camelia, rose and other flowers. The wreath was a gift to Elizabeth Dalton Perry and William Hayes Perry. At the time of this photograph the wreath belonged to the Perrys' nephew John H. Crum of Santa Monica. The shell wreath is an example of Victorian era domestic crafts.

title_tesim: Cast of "O.K. By Me" directed by Louise Glaum, Los Angeles, 1952
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Louise Glaum, 1952, O.K. by Me, Play, Louise Glaum.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This production was referenced by two  Los Angeles Times articles, "Stage Farce Offered at Beaux Arts Theater." 11 Oct.  1952, and "Beaux Arts Will Reopen." 19 Sep. 1952.

Louise Glaum was the director of this production of "O.K. By Me," a play by Sheldon Shepard. Mrs. Glaum was an actress, drama teacher and director, who was active in the Los Angeles area for over 60 years. Originally known as one of the first "vamp" actresses, she later opened several theaters in Los Angeles and an acting school.

This image is of the cast of "O.K. by Me", likely including (L to R): Peggy O'Connell (L), Stanley Herbert Briggs, Judith Evans, Wally Norman, Margaret Wells, Otis Garth, Louise Glaum, an unidentified man, and pearl Jenkins.

title_tesim: Santa Monica welfare center workers Marie Finley and Betty Shoemaker with quilting squares, Santa Monica, 1934
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2645 P1 to 3, 10/10/34. Welfare Center sewing room. SM 1934. Marie Finley left. Betty Shoemaker (Right) with blanket.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 3645 P1
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of Marie Finley, left, and Betty Shoemaker, workers at the Santa Monica welfare center holding several six-pointed quilting squares. The squares, purported to be 100 years old, were donated to the center.

The Santa Monica welfare center appears to be affiliated with the Santa Monica Welfare Council. A solicitation for clothing donations (referencing both “the Santa Monica welfare center” and “the council”) appeared in the Santa Monica Evening Outlook article, “Welfare Council Wants Clothes,” 3 Jun. 1927:1.

title_tesim: Gordone De Lagniel at the Ebell Club, Los Angeles, 1942
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Miss Gordone D'Lagniel [spell], Donoho, 3100 west Adams St, Child Estate 6/26/42, Shakespere [spell].
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This photograph is probably related to the article "Ebell Juniors Slate Carnival and Dance; Bishop Stevens Will Discuss Shakespeare." 18 Apr. 1942: A6.

Gordone De Lagniel (born Mayes Dickens Donoho and also known as Maggie), was a singer and socialite, active in the 1940s and 1950s. She is mentioned performing singing in various L.A. Times articles during this period. A 1941 city directory for Los Angeles lists her as Gordone DeLagniel, and gives her profession as Musician, and her address as 3100 West Adams Blvd.

Gordone De Lagniel at the Ebell dressed to  perform Shakespearean songs for the annual luncheon to celebrate the birthday of Shakespeare. She is wearing a Shakespearean-inspired costume with an ostrich feather-trimmed hat, a fur-trimmed shawl, gown and parasol. Behind her is flight of stairs. The Ebell is located at 743 S. Lucerne Blvd.

description_tesim: This image is of newspaper clippings for the play "O.K. by Me", directed by Louise Glaum. There are various articles, including two from the Los Angeles  Times, "Stage Farce Offered at Beaux Arts Theater." 11 Oct. 1952, and "Beaux Arts Will Reopen." 19 Sep. 1952. The list of the cast of characters can also be seen in the center of the page, as can two images of Mrs. Glaum and one of the cast.

Louise Glaum was the director of this play. Mrs. Glaum was an actress, drama teacher and director, who was active in the Los Angeles area for over 60 years. Originally known as one of the first "vamp" actresses, she later opened several theaters in Los Angeles and an acting school.

description_tesim: Negative misfiled.

Workers in a Santa Monica lumber yard. The men are seen holding hammers and saws. Most wear hats, work shirts and pants; two wear overalls. Four of the men look towards the camera while three continue working. In the yard, wood planks are stacked at foreground while the remaining area is piled with salvaged building materials, including window and door frames (at right).  The lumber yard is located near the Santa Monica Ice and Cold Storage Company, recorded as being located on 12th and Colorado, Santa Monica.

description_tesim: P. H. Philbin, of Chicago, a former owner of the Sorrento Beach Club building, had planned a 12-story apartment building on the site in 1953. The project included utilizing the massive concrete Sorrento Beach Club structure which had been abandoned in an unfinished state. The project was controversial, as some property owners felt that it would mar the beauty of the Santa Monica Palisades Park. Philbin may have had this image taken to document the Palisades area near the Sorrento Beach Club.
This image is a view of the cliffs at Palisades Park, Santa Monica and the highway and beach below. In the background, the Santa Monica Pier can be seen.

P. H. Philbin, of Chicago, a former owner of the Sorrento Beach Club building on the Pacific Coast Highway at the base of the palisades cliffs, had planned a 12-story apartment building on the site in 1953. The project included utilizing the massive concrete Sorrento Beach Club structure which had been abandoned in an unfinished state. The project was controversial, as some property owners felt that it would mar the beauty of the Santa Monica Palisades Park.

title_tesim: “Lucia di Lammermoor” production with Betty Herrick and Giovanni Zavatti, John Adams Auditorium, Santa Monica, 1951
caption_tesim: Handwritten on negative sleeve: 16 Lucia
Text from negative sleeve: 1951. Lucia. Opera. Civic Opera. Betty Herrick. Natalie & Kay Mazet.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of a Santa Monica Civic Opera  performance of “Lucia di Lammermoor” with Betty Herrick as Lucia in a bridal gown (L), Giovanni Zavatti (facing Lucia) and others. The singers wear 17th century period costume, the women in gowns, the men (with the exception of two) in satin breeches and doublet-inspired tunics, ruff collars, and baldricks worn diagonally across their chest (to hold their swords). The man standing closest to Lucia wears a more austere, dark costume with a wide flat collar and cuffs; the singer to his right wears a jerkin, short breeches, cavalier boots and a baldric. The set represents a hall in Lammermoor Castle with walls, stairs and a doorway. Props include a table and chair. Musicians in the orchestra pit are viewed in foreground. Likely performed at John Adams Junior High School, Santa Monica.

title_tesim: “Lucia di Lammermoor” production with Betty Herrick and Giovanni Zavatti, John Adams Auditorium, Santa Monica, 1951
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 1951. Lucia. Opera. Civic Opera. Betty Herrick. Natalie & Kay Mazet.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Betty Herrick as Lucia and Giovanni Zavatti as Edgardo in “Lucia di Lammermoor.” Herrick wears a gown emulating 17th century attire. It is comprised of a full skirt, lace overlay bodice with long sleeves, and a tie around the waist. An organza shawl and bejeweled necklace and earrings complete the ensemble. Zavatti wears a jerkin, short full breeches, cavalier boots and a baldric with sword. They stand in front of a flat background with a painted fountain and exterior architecture representing Lammermoor Castle. The dimly lit stage suggests nighttime. The conductor’s podium, musical scores, and a musician can be seen from the orchestra pit at foreground. Likely performed at John Adams Junior High School, Santa Monica.

title_tesim: “Lucia di Lammermoor” production with Betty Herrick, John Adams Auditorium, Santa Monica, 1951

description_tesim: Opera singer Betty Herrick as Lucia in a Santa Monica Civic Opera of “Lucia di Lammermoor.” Herrick as Lucia stands at center while the cast lines up along the set’s periphery to watch her. The cast wears costumes evoking 17th century attire, the women in gowns, the men in satin breeches and doublet-inspired tunics, ruff collars, and baldrics worn diagonally across their chest (to hold their swords). Lucia wears a smock under her satin robe. The setting is of a hall in Lammermoor Castle with paneled walls and a stairway. Props include a table and chair and chandelier hanging overhead. The conductor’s podium, musical scores and musicians are viewed in the orchestra pit at foreground. Likely performed at John Adams Junior High School, Santa Monica.

description_tesim: In this photo, the mock-up rests atop floral print paper seen at the bottom of the image. In a duplicate photo the paper has been removed. See image ark no. 21198/zz002d4f5q.

Mock-up manuscript page titled, “Impressions of the Opera,” by Adelbert Bartlett. The article describes a Santa Monica Civic Opera production of “Lucia di Lammermoor,” giving special attention to the performance by Betty Herrick in the title role. As with the article, most of the accompanying photographs feature Herrick. The article was intended for publication.

description_tesim: This image is of a program for the production "Liliane". Pictured on it are Lee Banning Morris and Don Shaw. The program reads: "Romantic Ballad, Lyrics by Lee Banning; Music by Don Shaw, 'Lee Banning and Don Shaw have a terriffic song in 'Liane'...it drew sensational applause when played and sung on the City of Hope Telethon.' From Barney Glazer's syndicate column."

Mrs. Lee Banning Morris worked in advertising at Los Angeles Advertising Women, Inc. She also volunteered at several L.A. County hospitals, including the V.A. hospital. She helped to recruit Liberace to perform at these hospitals for patients. Don Shaw was a musician who played in different bands in the Los Angeles area, including "The Don Shaw Trio," and "Don Shaw and The Ranch Hands."

title_tesim: Dean Cornwell, painter, standing before the Los Angeles Central Library, Los Angeles, 1932
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2334 p 1 to 14, 1932, Los Angeles Library, Dean Cornwell, Artist, 206 E. 33rd St NY city, Los Angeles Library.
named_subject_tesim: Los Angeles Public Library. Central Library
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections

ark:/21198/zz002d4mdr (document)
ark:/21198/zz002d4mf8  (document)
description_tesim: Dean Cornwell was an American painter and illustrator who was prominent in the early 20th century. His works include illustrations for Harper's Bazaar, Cosmopolitan, and Good Housekeeping. He also painted several other murals besides those for the Los Angeles Public (Central) Library, including for the locations: the Warwick Hotel New York, 10 Rockefeller Plaza New York, and the Sevier State Office Building in Tennessee. He is mentioned in several L.A. Times articles, including one dated June 21, 1931, which covers his research process for the murals for the Los Angeles Public (Central) Library.

Portrait of Dean Cornwell standing in of the front of the Los Angeles Public (Central) Library. At the time he was working on his mural, the "Four Great Eras of California History," located on the walls beneath the rotunda. Cornwell is wearing a suit and tie, holding a briefcase in one hand. In his other hand is a lit cigarette.
combined_subject_ssim: Los Angeles Public Library. Central Library

title_tesim: Cast members in a theatrical performance, Robert Major School of Acting, Beverly Hills, 1949
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Robert Major school, 1949
Handwritten on edge of negative: 13

description_tesim: Several members of the Robert Majors School of Acting performing in a play. Pairs of men and women look accusingly toward a young man standing over a woman who is kneeling at his feet. A man on the far right gestures toward him and appears to be speaking to another male wearing an overcoat and hat. The setting is that of a kitchen in dwelling. Torn wall paper exposes the brick wall beneath. A stove and sink are visible on the right. Also visible are curtained windows and a door (a coat hangs next to it on the right). Additional set props include a light-colored hutch (on the far right), a linen-covered table and chairs. Above the stage, an “R” can be seen inscribed within decorative molding. Several audience members can be seen beyond the stage at foreground.
The Robert Major School of acting was located in Beverly Hills, as noted in directories from 1929 and 1930.

title_tesim: John Burroughs Jr. High School play, Los Angeles, 1937
caption_tesim: Written on edge of negative: C W 37
Text from negative sleeve:  Home Rooms. Fall 1937. 2949. John Burroughs Jr. High School, 10/13/37. Play. Good for magazine.
named_subject_tesim: John Burroughs Junior High School (Los Angeles, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Four performers in a John Burroughs Jr. High School play. They are dressed in 17th century attire and are most likely portraying early settlers (possibly Jamestown or Plymouth). The two females wear bonnets, dresses with aprons tied at the waist, and scarves around their necks. One is seated at a spinning wheel seen with a spool of yarn is seen wound on a spindle. The other holds a book in her lap. A male dressed as a soldier stands over her. He holds a long firearm in one hand and wears a morion helmet and a breast plate over his doublet (jacket). The second male rests one hand on the chair of the girl at the spinning wheel.  He holds his hat with the other hand and wears a cloak, jacket, breeches and stockings. The setting is the interior of a log dwelling with two doors and a window (next to a wall mounted plate rack). Additional props include a table with carafe and cups on a serving tray and a wooden bucket.
combined_subject_ssim: John Burroughs Junior High School (Los Angeles, Calif.)

description_tesim: Four performers in a John Burroughs Jr. High School play. They are dressed in 17th century attire and are most likely portraying early settlers (possibly Jamestown or Plymouth). The two females wear bonnets, dresses with aprons tied at the waist, and scarves around their necks. One is seated at a spinning wheel seen with a spool of yarn is seen wound on a spindle. The setting is the interior of a log dwelling with two doors and a window (next to a wall mounted plate rack). description_tesim: Four performers in a John Burroughs Jr. High School play. They are dressed in 17th century attire and are most likely portraying early settlers (possibly Jamestown or Plymouth). The two females wear bonnets, dresses with aprons tied at the waist, and scarves around their necks. One is seated at a spinning wheel seen with a spool of yarn is seen wound on a spindle. The standing male is dressed as a soldier. He holds a long firearm in one hand and a morion helmet in the other, and wears a breast plate over his doublet (jacket). The seated male wears a cloak, jacket, breeches and stockings, a Pilgrim’s hat rest on his thighs. The setting is the interior of a log dwelling with a stone fireplace. Additional props include a large wooden bench, a wooden bucket (at right), a kettle that hangs inside of the fireplace, and a pitcher of flowers atop the mantle.
combined_subject_ssim: John Burroughs Junior High School (Los Angeles, Calif.)

description_tesim: Marjorie Gestring won the gold medal in the 3-meter springboard dive at the 1936 Berlin Olympics. Gestring was 13 years of age, becoming the youngest person ever to win the gold medal.

Photograph of Los Angeles High School students wearing graduation caps and gowns. Marjorie Gestring, 1936 Olympic gold-medalist, is viewed miming a signature in the yearbook she holds. She is seated with two unidentified female students. The yearbook’s pages are open to a shot of the school’s façade and tower. Photographed on the bleachers at Los Angeles High School’s athletic field. The photograph was meant to be used in publicity for the school.
combined_subject_ssim: Los Angeles High School (Los Angeles, Calif.)

title_tesim: View toward lighthouse building from Palisades cliffs above Will Rogers Beach, Los Angeles, 1940-1946
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 2890 P 4
Text from negative sleeve: 2890 P 4 to 9. Huntington Palisades, Pacific Palisades, Santa Monica area above Will Rogers State Beach Lighthouse. See 2890 P 1 for best negative.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
subject_tesim: Will Rogers State Beach (Los Angeles, Calif.)
description_tesim: Photograph view facing north toward the Santa Monica Bay and lighthouse building on Will Rogers State Beach, taken atop a Palisades cliff. A portion of Pacific Coast Highway is seen bottom left; a cactus grows with shrubbery on the cliffs at right.
The lighthouse building was purchased by Will Rogers in 1928 and eventually demolished in 1978. It was not a functioning lighthouse. Co-ordinates derived from original lighthouse location.

This image appears to pre-date a photograph of Caroline Bartlett taken on this spot in 1946. The image of Bartlett shows an additional structure (possibly a lifeguard tower) on the landing next to the lighthouse. The lighthouse tower has also gained an additional sign advertising "Seafood" next to "Swimsuits." (See image ark no. 21198/zz002d86c7)
combined_subject_ssim: Will Rogers State Beach (Los Angeles, Calif.)

title_tesim: Two couples dressed in Persian attire, Del Mar Club, Santa Monica, 1942
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2896 P 1-8. Del Mar Club, Santa Monica, 1942. Fashion Show, Opera La Tosco [sic] 10/20/42. Julian Oliver 1725 N. Bronson, 1 set 8x10 DW Holly.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 2896 P 8
named_subject_tesim: Casa del Mar Hotel (Santa Monica, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Image of two couples dressed in Persian attire. One man stands and speaks while the others sit low to the floor and listen. Both men wear elaborate turbans (one with a plumed turban, the other bejeweled). They wear long embroidered khalats (robes) over stripped sirwals (baggy trousers), and curled-toe slippers. Both women wear shear veils, but the woman on the left appears to be wearing a skirt or dress, while the woman on the right wears a beaded blouse with harem pants and gold-tone sandals. Swaths of fabric are seen strewn on the floor surrounding the group. Several potted plants complete the setting. In the background, the room’s walls are covered in tufted fabric. Photographed in a room at the Del Mar Club, Santa Monica.
combined_subject_ssim: Casa del Mar Hotel (Santa Monica, Calif.)

title_tesim: Annual Spanish Fiesta, Memorial Greek Amphitheatre, Santa Monica, 1949
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2897 P1-2. Santa Monica High School. Greek theatre. 1949.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 2897 P1
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Men in matador costumes reenact a bullfighting scene at the annual Spanish Fiesta. The stage is a mock courtyard complete with fountain. The audience watches from stadium seats and from the platform stage behind the performers where there is a two-story replica of a Spanish-style residence. As three others look on, one matador kneels with back turned away from the bull (two people in costume). The words, “Viva La Fiesta” are posted at the platform base. Spanish Fiesta, held at Santa Monica High School Greek Theatre.

description_tesim: Dancers in Spanish style dresses and veils perform at the annual Spanish Fiesta. The stage is a mock courtyard complete with fountain. The audience watches from stadium seats and from the platform stage behind the performers where there is a two-story replica of a Spanish-style residence. People watch from a window and stand at a wall on the second floor, while dozens more watch from seating on the stage.  The words, “Viva La Fiesta” are posted at the platform base. Photographed at Santa Monica High School Greek Theatre.

title_tesim: View of the Pacific Ocean through eucalyptus trees, Huntington Palisades, Pacific Palisades, 1940-1946
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 2890 P 9
Text from negative sleeve: 2890 P 4 to 9. Huntington Palisades, Pacific Palisades, Santa Monica area above Will Rogers State Beach Lighthouse. See 2890 P 1 for best negative.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph is one of a succession of photos labeled 2890 P 4-9. The estimated creation date for photographs labeled P 4 and P 5 is 1940-1946, based on the date of a third image taken at the same location (see image ark nos. 21198/zz002d4xkp, 21198/zz002d4xm6, and 21198/zz002d86c7).

Huntington Palisades is a neighborhood situated on the bluffs north of Santa Monica Canyon. The land was first purchased by Abbott Kinney in 1887. Kinney planted eucalyptus trees with the intention of using the wood for railroad ties. When that proved to be untenable, he sold the entire property. Subsequent landowners, including Collis Huntington and Robert C. Gillis, further developed the land into residential lots. The development was eventually completed by Mark Daniels and W.W. Williams, with the first home built on the property in 1926.

Photograph of a grassy area populated with several eucalyptus trees, with the Pacific Ocean in the background. Additional trees and shrubs can be seen behind a brick wall on the left. The property was probably located on the bluffs above Santa Monica Canyon, in the Huntington Palisades neighborhood.

title_tesim: Sketches of the Yukon River at sunset, 1910
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 1910. Yukon River. Copies of sketches.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Two color sketches representing the Yukon River’s varied landscapes at sunset. At top: The sun sets over the Yukon River, its rays radiating to the image edge and illuminating the floodplain at foreground. Bottom: Bartlett continues to manipulate the natural landscape, placing white glaciers at foreground while manipulating the sky above - here comprised of blue, green and pink color bands. A deep fuchsia ribbon floats in from above and out to the left of the image. Adelbert Bartlett, artist.

title_tesim: “Lucia di Lammermoor” scene performed during "One Night at the Opera," Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, 1950
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Nite [sic] of opera. 163 to 170.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The ecerpt from “Lucia di Lammermoor” was performed as part of “One Night at the Opera,” an event showcasing scenes from the association’s most popular operas. The performance took place January 28, 1950 at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.

Singers with the Santa Monica Civic Opera performing Act 2, Scene 2 from “Lucia di Lammermoor.” At center stage stands Enrico, portrayed by Enrico Porta, flanked by Edgardo (Raymond Gagan at left in tall boots, fitted breeches and shirt) and Raimondo (Richard Kissler). Enrico and Raimondo both wear satin doublets with high collars and breeches with stockings. Lucia (Natalie Garrotto) stands off to their right. She wears a wedding gown and veil. She is being comforted by her flanked by her handmaid, Alisa (Catherine Mazet). Cast members portraying wedding attendees watch as events transpire. The setting is a hall in Lammermoor Castle. Upstage, a stair case and narrow wall panels suggest a grand room. Downstage, two chairs and a table topped with quill pen and ink are the only props visible. The entire stage, orchestra pit, and numerous audience members are visible. Photograph taken from balcony.

description_tesim: An excerpt from “La Traviata” was performed as part of “One Night at the Opera,” an event showcasing scenes from the association’s most popular operas.

Dancers perform “the gypsy dance,” in "La Traviata," Act 2, Scene 2. The dancers take center stage in Romani (Gypsy) attire. Female cast members portraying party attendees are dressed in gowns, the men in dress suits and cravats. They stand on the sidelines watching the dance. The stage set is comprised of gated stone walls surrounded by trees, replicating the courtyard of a country home outside of Paris. Musicians are viewed in the orchestra pit at foreground.

title_tesim: Don Shaw Trio with Lee Banning Morris, Sarnez Restaurant, Los Angeles, 1952
caption_tesim: Written on edge of negative: #1
Text from negative sleeve: Don Shaw Musical Trio. 12/29/52. Don Shaw. CR 62471. 554 Huntley Dr. c/o Lee Banning Morris. 141-B Reves [sic] Dr. Bev Hills

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
ark:/21198/zz002d5dqz (document)
description_tesim: Don Shaw Trio publicity photograph with Mrs. Lee Banning Morris. From left to right: Mrs. Morris, Don Shaw, and two unidentified musicians from the Trio. Shaw’s guitarist leans on the piano with a microphone in the crook of his arm, while the bass player holds a maraca in one hand, his upright bass with the other. Photographed at Sarnez Restaurant, 107 N. La Cienega Blvd.

Don Shaw was a musician who played in different bands in the Los Angeles area, including "The Don Shaw Trio," and "Don Shaw and The Ranch Hands." Mrs. Lee Banning Morris co-wrote the ballad, "Liliane" with Shaw. She wrote the lyrics and he contributed the music.
Related correspondence from Lee Banning Morris to Adelbert Bartlett gives location of photo shoot (see "Relation" note).

description_tesim: The performance billed as “One Night at the Opera” was an event showcasing scenes from the association’s most popular operas. The presentation took place January 28, 1950 at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.

Collage of newspaper clippings and ephemera regarding the Santa Monica Civic Opera Association’s 1949-1950 season. Its most popular productions, “Lucia di Lammermoor,” “Rigoletto” and “La Traviata,” are highlighted. The operas were three of the association’s most popular productions. Excerpts from these operas were performed in the production, “One Night at the Opera.” Playbills for “Lucia di Lammermoor” and “One Night at the Opera” are included.

title_tesim: Performers in a children's concert, Roosevelt Elementary, Santa Monica, 1950s
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Children's Concert.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of children in a school performance. The students range in age and are all girls with the exception of one boy at right. The girls wear a variety of costumes and uniforms. At ground level, nine young ladies wear Intermediate Girl Scout uniforms with neckerchiefs and button-down fronts. On stage, groups of dancers represent different cultures and styles of dance. From left to right: a teacher stands next to three girls wearing peasant blouses with full skirts and embellished hats; two wear graphic print pants with loose button-down shirts; one girl wears a modern ballerina ensemble. They are followed by Polynesian Hula girls in "grass" skirts and satin bikini tops, and four ballroom dancers - the boy wearing a tuxedo with a short jacket. Photographed from the audience, with several seated audience members in view. Photographed at Roosevelt Elementary School, Santa Monica.

title_tesim: Daralyn, Janis and Sharisse Milne at Christmas time, Santa Monica, circa 1957 or 1958

description_tesim: A copy print portrait of the girls’ mother, Norma J. Milne, is included in the collection. The family's Santa Monica address is noted on the negative sleeve, 3329 Kansas Ave. (see image ark no. 21198/zz002d5r61).

Photograph of Daralyn, Janis and Sharisse Milne (left to right), seated on a rocker recliner in front of a Christmas tree decorated with tinsel and ornaments. Wrapped gifts can be seen on the floor in the background. The two eldest girls wear matching dresses. All three girls hold books. The eldest looks at the camera while the middle sister reads; the youngest looks up at a female partially viewed at left. She is probably their mother, Norma Milne. The girls were photographed at their family home in Santa Monica.

description_tesim: Elfie A. Moss was Santa Monica’s first City Librarian (1890 to 1939). Santa Monica was declared a town in the same year that Mosse’s home was built.

Photograph of a two-story Victorian style home once owned by Elfie A. Mosse of Santa Monica, located at 1433 5th St. The house has clapboard siding, a shingled roof, sash windows (seen on second floor), and a portico with dentil molding capping the columns seen on the porch’s right side. Willow trees and an assortment of plantings hide much of the façade. At foreground, a chicken can be seen behind a short gate made of wood and chicken wire.

title_tesim: "Rigoletto" production curtain call with Ray Gagan, Enrico Porta, Natalie Garrotto and others, John Adams Auditorium, Santa Monica, 1949

description_tesim: Five opera singers with the Santa Monica Civic Opera stand holding hands at curtain call. From left: a male and female in Romani (Gypsy) dress portray Sparafucile (a hired assassin) and his sister, Maddalena. To their left, Raymond Gagan as the Duke of Mantua and Enrico Porta as Rigoletto are dressed in more Renaissance style attire, wearing doublet-inspired tunics with belts at the waist, stockings, and short breeches (called slops); Natalie Garrotto (as Gilda) is dressed in the guise of a man and wears a belted tunic and boots. An interior backdrop is obscured by stage curtains. Musicians are viewed in the orchestra pit at foreground.

description_tesim: The Santa Monica Civic Opera performed “Rigoletto” at the John Adams Auditorium November 26-27, and December 3-4. Enrico Porta played Rigoletto for all four show

Enrico Porta, in costume as the title character, “Rigoletto,” for a performance of the Santa Monica Civic Opera, photographed with three performance attendees. One wears a fur coat, the other two in contemporary overcoats. Porta wears a stripped doublet-inspired tunic, full, short breeches (called slops), stockings and a cape. The women are separated from Porta by a stone wall stage set covered with vines, and a garden bench.

description_tesim: Photograph of four cast members, two males and two females, in costume, from the Santa Monica Civic Opera. From left: Louis Statham, performing as Sparafucile, wears a Gypsy or pirate-style outfit with a loose shirt, vest, pirate pants and boots. The outfit is completed with a scarf tied around his head and an earring in one ear, and a knife tucked into his sash. Two females wear satin gowns with fitted bodices, lace collars and puffed sleeves. A Malcolm Laurin is dressed as Count Monterone. He wears an embellished tunic with a belt. Photographed backstage next to curtain hoists.
The Santa Monica Civic Opera performed “Rigoletto” at the John Adams Auditorium November 26-27, and December 3-4.

description_tesim: Photograph of "Hull House," sometime before its demolition in the early 1950s. The two-story, wood-frame structure is surrounded by trees. A swing seat sits on a covered porch with lattice work enclosure at left. Right of the entryway are three sets of multi-paned windows. A second floor balcony sits atop the porch. Wooden shutters cover the remaining windows on both floors. Numerous cars are parked at left of the home, while three other dwellings are seen in the background at left and right.

Negative sleeve notes location of Hull House as being between Pico and the next street south on Appian Way. Appian Way becomes Ocean Ave. south of the Appian/Pico intersection; the next street south of Pico is Bay Street. Ocean front properties in the area surrounding Pico Boulevard include Shutters on the Beach and Hotel Casa del Mar.

title_tesim: Mission style building on Magnolia Avenue, Fontana, 1928-1931
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 2104
Text from negative sleeve: 2098… 2102–2103–2104–2105–2106–2107–2108–2109–2110–2111–2112. Fontana. Chickens – Corrals. [stamped:] Adelbert Bartlett Los Angeles
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Fontana was founded in 1913 by A.B. (Azarial Blanchard) Miller, d. 1878-1941. Miller first purchased the 17,000 acres of land in 1906, a year after he began working it. Miller envisioned a booming agricultural town complete with citrus orchards, cattle and poultry farms situated along Route 66. With the formation of the Fontana Land Company in 1914 and Fontana Farms Company in 1918, Miller’s vision began to come to fruition. The Los Angeles Times printed numerous articles regarding A.B. Miller, Fontana Farms and Fontana Land companies from 1914 to 1937; articles specific to poultry farming and egg production date from 1925 to 1931.

Mission style building on Magnolia Avenue, half a mile from Foothill Boulevard (Route 66). Signs in the window read California Glass Company. What appears to be large coils (possibly wire) can be viewed through the windows. Cars from the 1920s are parked along two streets. An Auto Club street sign directs drivers to Inland Empire cities and to Los Angeles. Flowers, grass and trees are planted along the streets, while power lines crisscross over the building toward two utility poles on the left.  A man wearing a suit and bowler hat walks toward the poles away from view. Photographed from a cattycorner lot in Fontana.

Photograph of four girls between a row of flowers and an irrigation trench that runs along a residence. The girls wear similar jumpers and sport matching bobbed haircuts. The youngest girl holds a straw hat while the other three wear cloche hats. Two stand and two sit along the trench’s edge. The house behind them is clad with wood siding and has a brick chimney. Other dwellings can be seen in the distant background. Photographed on a tract of land in Fontana.

description_tesim: Photograph of the Fontana Farms Company Land Sales Office, situated in a mission style building. The building’s 5-story tower includes small lookout windows on three floors and iron-work surrounding open arch-ways. A small courtyard near the entry, covered pathways, tiled roofs and a stripped awning on one of the windows completes the mission style look. The landscape if populated by young plantings. An early 30s model car sits in the driveway. Photographed in Fontana.

Fontana was founded in 1913 by A.B. (Azarial Blanchard) Miller, d. 1878-1941. Miller first purchased the 17,000 acres of land in 1906, a year after he began working it. Miller envisioned a booming agricultural town complete with citrus orchards, cattle and poultry farms situated along Route 66. With the formation of the Fontana Land Company in 1914 and Fontana Farms Company in 1918, Miller’s vision began to come to fruition. The Los Angeles Times printed numerous articles regarding A.B. title_tesim: Political cartoon commenting on Congressional Representative Mark S. Hogue, 1954
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Cartoons, 1954. Oil. Montana Ave Laundries. Oil.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: For duplicate drawing see image ark no. 21198.zz002d5pzf

Reverend Mark S. Hogue was elected as 16th District Congressional Representative on November 2, 1954. He was endorsed by the International Longshoremen’s and Warehouseman’s Union, which was headed by Harry Bridges. Bridges was identified as a member of the Communist Party by several witnesses appearing before government agencies and investigating committees.

Photograph of a political cartoon drawn by Adelbert Bartlett. The drawing shows a man walking along a path. At left, a griffin flies over head with “LEFT-WING” printed on its wing. Farther along the path at right is the depiction of an implied crucifixion (as illustrated by “GOLGOTHA” written above three inferred crosses). The cross at center is being constructed with the help of a rope and pulley – the pulley being a diamond pendant. Beneath the cross is a posted flyer referring to Rev. Mark S. Hogue, 16th District Representative, and Democrat. The bill pronounces: “Hogue, yesterday’s candidate, unwitting pawn, [un-American] factions, unsound advice, untenable promises. What is a man profited. if he gain the whole world. yet lose his own soul…?” [Text at bottom:] “16th congressional position already filled. [Text at left:] Coming Election Day. The Sacrifice. Vote early.”

title_tesim: Ballet performance by dancers of the Andrei Tremaine studio, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, 1955
caption_tesim: Handwritten on negative sleeve: 3
Text from negative sleeve: 10-9-55. Wm Tremaine, Andrei Tremaine. Studio Bundy & San Vicente.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Six ballet dancers wearing romantic-style tutus form a semi-circle around a couple performing downstage center. The male (possibly Andrei Tremaine) is executing a lift, with the ballerina on one shoulder, while she holds her arms in fifth position. They dance in front of a painted backdrop replete with trees atop rolling hills behind a wall with arched portals, and painted with flowers and foliage. Beyond the stage, two pianists are viewed in silhouette in the orchestra pit at foreground.

Andrei (William or “Bill”) Tremaine was a soloist with the Ballet Russe de Monte Carlo who went on to become principal dancer, choreographer and artistic director of the Pacific Ballet Dance Theatre (Burbank). Tremaine was also noted as an instructor in a Los Angeles Times article, “Girl, 11, Once Paralyzed by TB, in Ballet,” 21 Jul. 1955. His studio was likely located at Bundy and San Vicente.

title_tesim: "Ruddigore" production, (North Hollywood, probably), 1956
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 2
Text from negative sleeve: Oct '56. Mary Jesson, Valley opera, Ruddigore.

description_tesim: Mary Jesson, born Ethel Mary Jesson (1917-2006), was a performer with the Valley Savoyards and president of the Gilbert and Sullivan Society (affiliated with the D’Oyly Carte Company and various Gilbert and Sullivan performance groups), as noted in the Pasadena Independent, 19 Dec. 1966:11.

Formed in 1956, the Valley Savoyards opera company was devoted to performing works by Gilbert and Sullivan. (Devotees of Gilbert and Sullivan are called "Savoyards" after the Savoy Theatre in London, which was built specifically for the presentation of the pairs' operas). Notices for Valley Savoyards performances appear in the Los Angeles Times for only two years (1959-1960), with performances at locations in North Hollywood (most notably at the Assistance League Playhouse), and one performance at the Encino Community Center.

Opera singers performing in a Valley Savoyards production of "Ruddigore." Mary Jesson, performing as Rose Maybud, stands at the fore while a male performer sings (left of Jesson). Most cast members wear costumes evoking the 19th century, while "Rose Maybud" and three women portraying the "Professional Bridesmaids" wear long, light-colored bridal dresses with veils. Photographed on stage at an unidentified theatre, likely in North Hollywood (Los Angeles) or an adjacent San Fernando Valley locale.

Text from negative sleeve: 10-9-55. Wm Tremaine, Andrei Tremaine. Studio Bundy & San Vicente.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Andrei (William or “Bill”) Tremaine was a soloist with the Ballet Russe de Monte Carlo who went on to become principal dancer, choreographer and artistic director of the Pacific Ballet Dance Theatre (Burbank). Tremaine was also noted as an instructor in a Los Angeles Times article, “Girl, 11, Once Paralyzed by TB, in Ballet,” 21 Jul. 1955. His studio was likely located at Bundy and San Vicente.

Six ballet dancers wearing romantic-style tutus bow in formation while the prima ballerina (in a pancake tutu) stands under a spotlight at center. They dance in front of a painted backdrop replete with trees atop rolling hills behind a wall with arched portals, and painted with flowers and foliage. Two pianists are visible in the orchestra pit at foreground.

title_tesim: Opera singers performing “La Traviata” at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, Los Angeles, 1951
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Frank Pace. Opera Traviata, 9/15/51.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 24
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Verdi’s “La Traviata,” produced by Francesco (Frank) Pace, was performed at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, September 15, 1951. Several notices were published in the Los Angeles Times. A critique appeared in the article, “’La Traviata’ Staged on Verdi Anniversary,” 17 Sep. 1951: B8

Los Angeles Civic Grand Opera production of “La Traviata,” Act 2, Scene 2. Singer Gene Curtsinger, as Alfredo Germont, sits at a gambling table on the right. Opposite him is an unidentified performer in the role of Baron Douphol (wearing a monocle and sash across his chest). Behind him stands a singer portraying Flora. Additional cast members wearing gowns and coattails sing as a chorus while the two men tensely interact. The setting is a room in Flora’s Parisian home. The set’s design and props reflect an opulent space, including decorative wall treatments, a curtained window, upholstered chairs and settee, and a chandelier hanging above the center space. Beyond the stage, a musician can be viewed in the orchestra pit at foreground.

title_tesim: “O.K. By Me” cast members, directed by Louis Glaum, Los Angeles, 1952
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Louise Glaum Play Hollywood, 1952. Also Peggy O’ Connell, Otis Garth.

description_tesim: Louise Glaum produced and directed “O.K. By Me,” a comedy written by Sheldon Shedard. Glaum was an actress, drama teacher and director who was active in the Los Angeles area for over 60 years. Originally known as one of the first “vamp” actresses, she later opened several theaters in Los Angeles and an acting school.
Cast members from the play “O.K. By Me,” photographed on stage in character. Cast from left to right: Stanley Herbert Briggs, Judith Evans, Wally Norman and Margaret Wells.
This production was referenced in two Los Angeles Times articles: “Stage Farce Offered at Beaux Arts Theater,” 11 Oct. 1952, and “Beaux Arts Will Reopen,” 19 Sep. 1952.

description_tesim: Photograph of Teresa Kirbe, second from left, flanked by unidentified men, women and children. A poster board referencing a Santa Monica Civic Opera production of “Hansel and Gretel,” with Kirbe as the Witch, is held by a man in suit and tie and two females. At far left, next to Kirbe, is a man dressed as a clown making a balloon animal. A boy, dressed as a hobo, kneels in front in him; a second boy (wearing, in a poncho, sombrero and fake mustache) sits next to a headless mannequin. Additional costumed children can be viewed behind the podium at ground level. Photograph likely taken in Santa Monica.

Image of Teresa Kirbe on poster board was used in a newspaper article regarding a Santa Monica Civic Opera Production of “Hansel and Gretel,” November 22-23, 1957 at Barnum Hall. Adelbert Bartlett utilized the same image in a collage of articles for the same production. (see "Relation" note)

title_tesim: Ribbonwood resort with Santa Rosa Mountains in view, Mountain Center vicinity, 1937
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2803. Ribbonwood Resort. San Jacinto Mts Via Keen Camp Post Office. Wn S. Howell Jr. proprietor. Photos of Ribbonwood Trees, 10/1/37. Dr. St. Louis Estes. Jean Myras.
Handwritten along edge of negative: 2803 P
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
subject_tesim: Santa Rosa and San Jacinto Mountains National Monument (Calif.)
description_tesim: The Ribbonwood Resort, located at the base of the Santa Rosa Mountains, was owned and operated by W. S. (Wilson Stout) Howell, Jr. (1888-1967). Howell purchased 2000 acres of land at the base of the Santa Rosa Mountains from the Southern Pacific Railroad with the intention of farming, but when the Pines-to-Palms Highway opened in 1932, he decided to open a rest stop to capitalize on the traffic that ran through portions of his property. Soon after, he began to clear paths to trails and created picnic areas. Rustic-style cabins soon followed. By 1935, Howell’s land was a full-fledged resort. Howell’s intention was to offer a place where those in ill health could rest and recuperate in a natural environment. He believed that the mountain location helped him rebuild his health and that it could do the same for others. Howell sold his land in 1964. It was developed into the residential development of Spring Crest, which sits within the Santa Rosa – San Jacinto Mountains National Monument.

Photograph of the Ribbonwood resort, seated at the base of the Santa Rosa Mountains. At foreground stands a store constructed like a ramada, covered with ribbonwood rush. A ribbonwood tree stands between the two entrances; a second grows next to a ladder, viewed resting against the structure on the left-hand side with firewood. Walls and windows are dotted with signs promoting products. Chalk board signs advertising “Ice Cold Drinks,” and “Ice Milk Bars” rest next to a 7-Up sign propped against the structure. The store sits within a clearing surrounded by ribbonwood trees. Paths lead to cabins built to look like prairie houses with rush roofs, and to mountain paths above the resort.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Rosa and San Jacinto Mountains National Monument (Calif.)

subject_tesim: Santa Rosa and San Jacinto Mountains National Monument (Calif.)
description_tesim: The Ribbonwood resort, located at the base of the Santa Rosa Mountains, was owned and operated by W.S. (Wilson Stout) Howell, Jr., d.1888-1967. Howell purchased 2000 acres of land at the base of the Santa Rosa Mountains from the Southern Pacific Railroad with the intention of farming, but when the Pines-to-Palms Highway opened in 1932, he decided to open a rest stop to capitalize on the traffic that ran through portions of his property. Soon after, he began to clear paths to trails leading up to the Santa Rosa Mountains and created picnic areas. Rustic-style cabins soon followed. By 1935, Howell’s land was a full-fledged resort. Howell’s intention was to offer a place where those in ill health could rest and recuperate in a natural environment. He believed that the mountain location helped him rebuild his health and that it could do the same for others. Howell sold his land in 1964. It was developed into the town of Spring Crest, which sits within the Santa Rosa – San Jacinto Mountains National Monument.

Model Jean Myras, wearing a blouse and scarf, shorts, and t-strap heels, poses on a boulder surrounded by trees and shrubs. Clouds form over mountains in the background. Photographed on the property of the Ribbonwood resort.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Rosa and San Jacinto Mountains National Monument (Calif.)

title_tesim: Biltmore Hotel, exterior view, Santa Barbara, 1939
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2765 P11to P14. Santa Barbara, 1939. Sunsets Palms near Biltmore; also Biltmore Hotel.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Exterior view of the Biltmore Hotel with courtyard at center. Steps lead from the lawn to a patio area with umbrella covered tables. The space is enveloped by trees, shrubs and bushes. People can be seen within the courtyard and walking along the lawn out front. The property sits in a residential neighborhood with views of the mountains. The hotel was photographed from the street.

The Spanish Colonial Revival style building was designed by Reginald D. Johnson and opened in December 1927. The Santa Barbara Biltmore was acquired by Four Seasons Hotels in 1987. The hotel is currently called, Four Seasons Resort—The Biltmore Santa Barbara, but is generally known as The Biltmore.

caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 2508 P 1

subject_tesim: Santa Monica State Beach Park (Santa Monica, Calif.)
description_tesim: May be the same person as Eugene Harry Drake, resident of Santa Monica.

Photograph of Eugene d’Orange, wearing a keffiyeh and agal, with Valeska Radd (standing, also wearing a keffiyeh) and June Diebold. Eugene d’Orange is viewed writing in a tray of sand. Both women wear similar bathing suits with patches on the chest. Miss Radd’s patch reads: “Roger Cornells Athletic Girls;” “Colton” is stitched to the suit at the hip. Miss Diebold’s patch reads: “A Roger Co[rnell] Athlete.” The trio are seated and standing on the sands of a Santa Monica Beach, with additional beach goers and the coastline on view in background.
Image described in a related typescript document (see "Relation" note).

Text from related typescript document: Valeska Radd, Yugo-Slavian blond Beauty (standing, right; seated, left) with June Diebold, University of California co-ed, having their “fortune” told by Eugene d’Orange, Scotch-Spanish poet and artist of Santa Monica, California, who upon his return from a journey to the Levant, wears Arab head-dress as protection from the sun and is introducing a strange Oriental writing in a tray of sand which he interprets in answer to those seeking advice in love, “always an easy and favorite subject with me,” sayd d’Orange, who is the author of poems of passion.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica State Beach Park (Santa Monica, Calif.)

title_tesim: Calico ghost town main street, San Bernardino County, Barstow vicinity, copy print, 1929
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2590. Calico Mine. Mojave Desert, 1929.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Located in the Calico Mountains of the Mojave Desert, the town of Calico was the largest silver mining camp in California. The mine operated from 1881 until it was abandoned in 1896. The town remained largely deserted but for a few who remained until the end of borax mining in the region in 1907. Today, Calico is a State Historic Landmark (#782), and is a designated San Bernardino County Regional Park.

Copy print photograph of Calico mining camp (renamed Calico Ghost Town). The image shows a main street with wood- constructed buildings on either side. The fairway culminates at a church with steeple, standing at the base of a mountain (above which additional structures are visible).

title_tesim: “Carmen” production with June Moss and Catherine Mazet, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, 1956
caption_tesim: Handwritten on negative sleeve: 18
Text from negative sleeve: 1956. SM Opera. “Carmen” 10/13/56. June Moss.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Santa Monica Civic Opera singers performing in Act 1 of “Carmen.” June Moss and Catherine Mazet (standing fourth and fifth from left respectively) and fellow cast members wearing Andalusian attire (men dressed in the vaquero style wearing short jackets or vests and breeches with wide sashes and wide-brimmed hats; women wearing head scarves, peasant blouses and full skirts with fabric tied at the waist) sing as a chorus while Don José, a soldier, sits to the left of stage wearing his helmet and sword. He appears disinterested in the crowd’s activity. At left stands a scenery flat representing a tobacco factory. A second flat representing a guard house stands across the stage. A backdrop painted with additional buildings and an open plaza creates the appearance of a town square. Photographed from the balcony, providing a view of musicians and the conductor in the orchestra pit, and a few audience members in the front row. Performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.
A notice for this production appears in the Los Angeles Times, 14 Oct. 1956: E6.

title_tesim: “Carmen” production, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, 1956
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 1956. SM Opera. “Carmen” 10/13/56. June Moss.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Santa Monica Civic Opera singers performing in Act 1 of “Carmen.” Cast members dressed as soldiers stand at left; a man in a vaquero inspired outfit (short jacket and breaches connected with a wide sash at the waist, wide bow scarf-tie, and wide-brimmed hat) stands near them at the bridge (a stepped set piece constructed to look like stone). Across the stage, young audience members wearing paper helmets stand at attention in front of the guard house. Three adult males stand near them upstage. The setting is a town square in Seville, Spain. Scenery flats and backdrops are constructed and painted to represent the square. Photographed from the balcony, the orchestra pit seen in silhouette.  Performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.
A notice for this production appears in the Los Angeles Times, 14 Oct. 1956: E6.

title_tesim: “Merry Widow” rehearsal performance, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, possibly 1960
caption_tesim: Handwritten on Gevaert film box: Merry Widow, New Moon.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 32
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Possibly related to the article, “Civic Group Will Present ‘Merry Widow’,” Los Angeles Times, 12 Jun. 1960: WS5.

Santa Monica Civic Opera singers rehearing for a performance of “The Merry Widow,” Act 1. Natalie Garrotto performs as Natalie (Valencienne) with an unidentified male singer, likely in the role of Count Camille de Jolidon (Camille de Rosillon). At left stands a crew member wearing street clothes. The female performers wear evening gowns, while the men wear tuxedos and military dress uniforms. An additional cast member dressed as an attendant stands to the far right. He wears a powdered wig, full sleeve shirt and waistcoat.  The scene is a ball being held at the fictional Marsovian (Pontevedrian) embassy in Paris. Decorative set pieces with intricate floral designs flank a faux marble staircase. Visible stage props include a table and a settee. Above the stage, microphones can be seen hanging from behind a border curtain. Beyond the stage, musicians can be seen in the orchestra pit wearing casual clothing. They talk, read sheet music and watch the performers. A young boy can be seen sitting on the orchestra pit railing at foreground. Photographed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.
English language adaptations of “The Merry Widow” include several name changes. Here, names from the original Viennese work are noted in parentheses after the variation.

title_tesim: "Merry Widow" production, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, possibly 1960
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 37
Handwritten on Gevaert film box: Merry Widow, New Moon.

description_tesim: Santa Monica Civic Opera singers performing in “The Merry Widow,” Act 2. Standing center stage is a singer in the role of the widow Sonia Sadoya (Hannah Glawari). . She and the other female performers (including Natalie Garrotto portraying Natalie (Valencienne), seated at the table on the left) wear the traditional clothing of a fictional Slavic country, including kokoshnik-inspired headdresses, peasant blouses, full skirts with aprons and waist cinchers. The men wear tuxedos and military dress uniforms. Cast members stand and behind her as she sings. The setting is a garden at Hannah’s home. Scenery flats representing trees, a faux brick wall and a lattice-work structure all contribute to the outdoor setting. Additional props include a rattan table, chairs and settee. Above the set, three microphones hang visibly from behind a border curtain. Photographed from the balcony, providing a view of the conductor and musicians in the orchestra pit, along with two rows of audience members.
Possibly related to the article, “Civic Group Will Present ‘Merry Widow’,” Los Angeles Times, 12 Jun. 1960: WS5.
English language adaptations of “The Merry Widow” include several name changes. Here, names from the original Viennese work are noted in parentheses after the variation.

description_tesim: Santa Monica Civic Opera singers performing in “The Merry Widow,” Act 1. A female singer in the role of the widow Sonia Sadoya (Hanna Glawari) performs at center stage while male cast members wearing tuxedos and military dress uniforms parade past her. The scene is a ball being held at the fictional Marsovian (Pontevedrian) embassy in Paris, and the “ladies choice dance” is about to start. An additional cast member dressed as an attendant stands to the far right. He wears a powdered wig, full sleeve shirt, waistcoat, breaches and stockings. He stands next to an entryway designed with intricate floral patterns. A second entryway stands across the stage; both flank a faux marble staircase. Visible stage props include chairs and a settee. Above the stage, microphones can be seen hanging from behind a border curtain. Beyond the stage, musicians are viewed in silhouette in the orchestra pit at foreground.
Possibly related to the article, “Civic Group Will Present ‘Merry Widow’,” Los Angeles Times, 12 Jun. 1960: WS5.
English language adaptations of “The Merry Widow” include several name changes. Here, names from the original Viennese work are noted in parentheses after the variation.

Santa Monica Civic Opera singer Natalie Garrotto performs in “The Merry Widow,” Act 1. Garrotto, portraying Natalie (Valencienne), stands center stage while fellow cast members (wearing gowns, tuxedos and military dress uniforms) gather at various points on the stage and watch her. An additional cast member dressed as an attendant stands to the far right. He wears a powdered wig, full sleeve shirt, waistcoat, breaches and stockings. The scene is a ball being held at the fictional Marsovian (Pontevedrian) embassy in Paris. Decorative set pieces with intricate floral designs flank a faux marble staircase. Above the stage, microphones can be seen hanging from behind a border curtain. Musicians can be seen in the orchestra pit.

description_tesim: Santa Monica Civic Opera performers in a production of “The New Moon.” Male and female cast members gather near a male performer standing on a stepped platform situated upstage center. Several look towards him as he performs, but one figure is viewed walking away from him, past men wearing sailor uniforms (white pants, stripped shirts and short jackets) towards the front of the stage. Image photographed from the balcony, providing a view of the entire stage – including microphones set at the foot of the stage, along with musician in the orchestra pit at foreground.

title_tesim: “Elisir d’amore" production with Roger Hansen and June Moss, John Adams Auditorium, Santa Monica, 1951
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: SM Civic Opera, Pagliacci, Elixir, Cavalleria.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 34
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: “L’Elisir d’amore” was performed twice at John Adams Auditorium during the company’s 8th season:  October 13 and November 3, 1951.

Santa Monica Civic Opera performance of “L’Elisir d’amore.” Roger Hansen portraying Dr. Dulcamara stands at center wearing a top hat with peacock feather, cape and coat, waist coat (vest), striped cravat and sash, trousers and boots. He also holds a walking stick. He is surrounded by a crowd of soldiers and villagers, including Adina, portrayed by June Moss singing at right (in front of a group of women). The setting is a village square in Basque country, late 18th century. Scenery includes a painted backdrop with rolling hills and trees, and set pieces painted to look like stone structures. Opera performed at John Adams Auditorium.

title_tesim: “Elisir d’amore" production with Roger Hansen, John Adams Auditorium, Santa Monica, 1951
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 33
Text from negative sleeve: SM Civic Opera, Pagliacci, Elixir, Cavalleria.

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Opera singers performing in “L’Elisir d’amore.” A female singer portraying the character Adina sings at foreground. She is watched by Roger Hansen as Dr. Dulcamara, standing to her right. She wears the clothing of a Basque villager with a full skirt, lace-up bodice, and shawl over her head. He wears a top hat with peacock feather, coat and vest, striped cravat and sash, trousers and boots.  He also holds a walking stick. The setting is a village square in Basque country, late 18th century. Scenery includes a painted backdrop with rolling hills and trees, and set pieces painted to look like stone structures, along with tables and chairs. Opera performed at John Adams Auditorium.
“L’Elisir d’amore” was performed twice at John Adams Auditorium during the company’s 8th season:  October 13 and November 3, 1951

title_tesim: “Elisir d’amore" production with June Moss, John Adams Auditorium, Santa Monica, 1951
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 33
Text from negative sleeve: SM Civic Opera, Pagliacci, Elixir, Cavalleria.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: “L’Elisir d’amore” was performed twice at John Adams Auditorium during the company’s 8th season:  October 13 and November 3, 1951.

Opera singers performing in “L’Elisir d’amore.” At left, June Moss, in the role of Adina, is being serenaded by a singer in military uniform, likely playing the role of Belcore. Beyond him, wearing a hat, vest and breeches, stands Nemorino (performer unidentified). The setting is a village square in Basque country, late 18th century. Scenery includes a painted backdrop with rolling hills and trees, and set pieces painted to look like stone architecture, one of which stands in for a cantina complete with a table and chairs. Beyond the stage at foreground, musicians can be viewed in the orchestra pit. Opera performed at John Adams Auditorium.

The setting is a village square in Basque country, late 18th century. Scenery includes a painted backdrop with rolling hills and trees, and set pieces painted to look like stone architecture. Santa Monica High School Amphitheatre.
In the Adelbert Bartlett collection, June Moss is documented in Santa Monica Civic Opera productions from 1949 to 1958.
